title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text: Singapore’s tourism sector experienced a severe shock during the pandemic and entered an uneven recovery phase in the years that followed. Monthly patterns in visitor arrivals, hotel occupancy, length of stay, and hotel performance indicators all suggest distinct recovery stages. Static charts alone are insufficient for users who need to compare series, detect structural shifts, and assess what may happen next. This project therefore proposes an interactive visual analytics prototype to support exploration, clustering, and forecasting on one shared tourism time-series dataset.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text: Singapore’s tourism sector experienced a severe shock during the pandemic and entered an uneven recovery phase in the years that followed. Monthly patterns in visitor arrivals, hotel occupancy, length of stay, and hotel performance indicators all suggest distinct recovery stages. Static charts alone are insufficient for users who need to compare series, detect structural shifts, and assess what may happen next. This project therefore proposes an interactive visual analytics prototype to support exploration, clustering, and forecasting on one shared tourism time-series dataset.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
2. Problem Statement
Current discussions on tourism recovery often focus narrowly on whether visitor numbers have returned, without adequately examining seasonality, source-market differences, or the way recovery paths vary across tourism series. In particular, there is a lack of an interactive time-series framework that allows users to compare country-level arrivals, detect recovery regimes, and assess how future demand may evolve.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
3. Project Aim
The project aims to develop a visual analytics prototype based on monthly tourism time series, enabling users to understand tourism recovery at descriptive, structural, and forecasting levels. The prototype will integrate time-series exploration, time-series clustering, and forecasting analysis to explain how tourism recovery trajectories evolve and how selected demand series may move in the near future.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
4. The Data
The project data is sourced from CEIC and Singapore Tourism Board related series and is reliable in terms of data origin. The selected workbook contains monthly tourism indicators such as:

Visitor Arrivals
Visitor Arrivals by selected countries and regions
Hotel Room Occupancy Rate
Average Length of Stay
Number of Hotels
Total Room Revenue

The project uses a compiled tourism time-series workbook spanning December 2015 onward, with the main analytical focus on monthly series. Core variables include total visitor arrivals, arrivals from selected countries and regions such as China, hotel room occupancy rate, monthly average length of stay, number of hotels, and total room revenue. The app will use one common monthly dataset across all modules so that exploration, clustering, and forecasting remain consistent. Within that shared dataset, the forecasting module will treat country-level monthly visitor arrivals as the primary target series, while hotel occupancy, stay length, and room revenue will be used as supporting indicators for interpretation.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
5. Research Questions
The project focuses on the following questions:

How did Singapore’s tourism market differ across the pre-COVID, shock, and recovery periods?
Did the recovery of selected source markets move in tandem with the broader tourism market?
Can monthly tourism observations be grouped into meaningful recovery-state clusters?
How well can selected country-level visitor-arrivals series be forecast using a baseline method and a model-based approach?

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
6. Methodology and Analytical Approach
The project adopts three main analytical modules:

Time-series exploration to reveal trends, seasonality, and unusual shifts across target series.
Time-series clustering to group country-level arrivals trajectories into interpretable recovery-pattern clusters.
Forecasting to compare a baseline method against a model-based approach on selected country-arrivals series and evaluate holdout accuracy, while interpreting those forecasts against hotel and stay indicators.

These modules are designed to use the same monthly dataset so that users can move from description to structure to prediction without changing data context.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
7. Decision Tree and Random Forest
The decision tree module emphasizes interpretability by translating complex relationships into clear rules, such as identifying thresholds below which months are likely to fall into low occupancy states. The random forest module serves as a more robust nonlinear classifier for assessing variable importance and testing model stability on unseen data. Together, the two approaches support both explanation and validation. Preliminary prototype testing suggests that total visitor volume is the most stable key variable, while seasonality, length of stay, and China’s visitor share provide additional discriminating power.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
8. Data Visualisation Methods
The prototype will include time-series line charts, target-series comparison views, cluster scatter plots, cluster profile tables, forecast plots, and forecast accuracy tables. These visualizations are designed not merely to display results, but to reveal seasonal structure, regime shifts, recovery-state similarities, and the relative performance of competing forecasting methods.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
9. R Packages







Package
Description




tidyverse
Used for data cleaning, transformation, filtering, summarisation, and general data manipulation.


readxl
Used to import the original Excel dataset into R.


lubridate
Used to process date variables and create time-based fields.


ggplot2
Used to create line charts, clustering plots, and forecast visualisations.


plotly
Used to add interactivity to selected visualisations where needed.


DT
Used to display interactive data tables in the prototype.


cluster
Used for clustering analysis and cluster quality assessment such as silhouette scores.


factoextra
Used to visualise clustering results, elbow plots, and cluster profiles.


forecast
Used to fit baseline and model-based time-series forecasts such as seasonal naive, ETS, and ARIMA.


tidymodels
Used to support time-aware train/test splitting and the forecasting modelling workflow.


timetk
Used to support time-series exploration and modeltime-friendly forecasting workflows.


modeltime
Used to calibrate, compare, refit, and forecast time-series models in a unified workflow.


tsibble
Used to represent time-indexed series for decomposition and temporal analysis.


feasts
Used to derive decomposition and seasonal diagnostics before model fitting.

Used to combine multiple ggplot charts into one display layout.

title: Shiny App
Shiny App Guide
text:
The app now follows a three-module structure built on one shared tourism time-series workbook:

app/app.R - top-level navigation and shared reactive data loading.
app/R/data_utils.R - common time-series ingestion, feature engineering, and forecasting helpers.
app/R/mod_cluster_ui.R and app/R/mod_cluster_server.R - time-series clustering module.
app/R/mod_forecast_ui.R and app/R/mod_forecast_server.R - forecasting module.
The Time Series Explorer is embedded directly in app/app.R to keep the main comparison flow simple.

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
section: 1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis
text:
1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis


Show the code
monthly_clean <- raw_tbl %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(date),
    !is.na(avg_stay_monthly),
    !is.na(hotel_occ),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals_china)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    year = lubridate::year(date),
    month = lubridate::month(date),
    quarter = lubridate::quarter(date),
    period = case_when(
      date <= as.Date("2020-01-01") ~ "pre_covid",
      date <= as.Date("2021-12-01") ~ "covid_shock",
      TRUE ~ "recovery"
    ),
    china_share = visitor_arrivals_china / visitor_arrivals,
    china_share_pct = china_share * 100
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.6Handling extreme values


Show the code
stay_cap <- quantile(monthly_clean$avg_stay_monthly, probs = 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)

monthly_clean <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    avg_stay_monthly_capped = pmin(avg_stay_monthly, stay_cap),
    visitor_arrivals_millions = visitor_arrivals / 1000000,
    visitor_arrivals_china_thousands = visitor_arrivals_china / 1000
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.7Generate analysis fields


Show the code
occ_cuts <- quantile(monthly_clean$hotel_occ, probs = c(1/3, 2/3), na.rm = TRUE)

analysis_ready <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    cluster_z_visitor_arrivals = as.numeric(scale(visitor_arrivals)),
    cluster_z_china_share = as.numeric(scale(china_share)),
    cluster_z_hotel_occ = as.numeric(scale(hotel_occ)),
    cluster_z_avg_stay_monthly_capped = as.numeric(scale(avg_stay_monthly_capped)),
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = case_when(
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[1]] ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[2]] ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    ),
    hotel_occ_level_business = case_when(
      hotel_occ < 70 ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= 85 ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    )
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = factor(hotel_occ_level_tertile, levels = c("low", "medium", "high")),
    hotel_occ_level_business = factor(hotel_occ_level_business, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"))
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    dataset_split = if_else(row_number() <= floor(n() * 0.8), "train", "test")
  )

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Objective
This prototype follows the Chapter 19 and 20 workflow from R for Visual Analytics: first understand the monthly time-series pattern, then build and compare forecasting models on a holdout window, and finally refit the best model to generate forward forecasts. The common dataset is organised around monthly visitor arrivals by country, while hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and room revenue are used as supporting indicators to interpret whether demand recovery is reflected in tourism performance.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Data Contract
Shared arrivals backbone:

data/raw/visitor_arrivals_full_dataset.xlsx

Optional supporting tourism context:

data/raw/Name your insight (4).xlsx

For this revised forecasting prototype, the analytical scope is intentionally narrowed to monthly visitor-arrival series by country. This matches the instructor guidance and the Chapter 19/20 forecasting workflow more closely than mixing arrivals with hotel or revenue indicators. Hotel occupancy, stay length, number of hotels, and room revenue remain optional supporting context only.
Examples of eligible target series are:

Visitor Arrivals: China
Visitor Arrivals: Malaysia
Visitor Arrivals: India
Visitor Arrivals: Indonesia
Visitor Arrivals: Australia
Visitor Arrivals: Japan

series_catalog <- list_country_arrival_series(tourism_data$long_monthly)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Analytical Design
The prototype uses one baseline and one forecasting model:

Baseline: seasonal naive forecast
Forecasting model: auto ARIMA

The workflow is:

Select a monthly series.
Hold out the final 12 months as the test window.
Fit both forecasting approaches on the training data.
Compare RMSE, MAE, and MAPE on the same holdout window.
Visualise actual values and forecasts together.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Example Target Series
Visitor Arrivals: China is used here because it is one of the clearest country-level recovery indicators in the dataset and it shows strong shock, rebound, and seasonal dynamics.

example_label <- "Visitor Arrivals: China"

example_series <- prepare_forecast_series(
  tourism_data$long_monthly,
  example_label
)

forecast_results <- run_modeltime_forecast_workflow(
  series_df = example_series,
  horizon = 12
)

context_series <- prepare_country_context_panel(
  tourism_data$long_monthly,
  country_label = example_label
)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Quality Gates

The selected series must have at least 24 non-missing monthly observations.
The holdout horizon must leave at least 12 points for model fitting.
A baseline and at least one model-based forecast must be scored on the same testing window.
The forecasting module must keep the shared arrivals workbook as the target backbone while using the tourism workbook only for supporting context indicators.
The workflow must include contextual comparison and visual diagnostics before model comparison.

title: User Guide
text: This guide explains how to use the final three-module tourism analytics app.

title: User Guide
text: This guide explains how to use the final three-module tourism analytics app.

title: User Guide
text:
Step 1: Explore the Series
Open the Time Series Explorer tab to:

Choose a monthly series such as Visitor Arrivals: China or Hotel Room Occupancy Rate.
Inspect the recent trend with a line chart.
Confirm the unit and source metadata before moving to deeper analysis.

title: User Guide
text:
Step 2: Compare Recovery States
Open the Cluster Prototype tab to:

Select multiple country-level visitor-arrivals series.
Set the lookback window and choose k.
Decide whether each country trajectory should be normalized before clustering.
Review the scatter plot, silhouette score, and cluster profile table.

title: User Guide
text:
Step 3: Forecast Future Values
Open the Forecasting Prototype tab to:

Select a country-level visitor-arrivals series.
Compare that arrivals series with hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and room revenue in the context chart.
Set the holdout horizon.
Compare the seasonal-naive baseline with ETS and ARIMA.
Review the forecast plot, decomposition views, and accuracy table.

title: User Guide
text:
Interpretation Tips

Use the explorer first to understand seasonality and structural breaks.
Use clustering second to identify which country-arrival trajectories behave similarly.
Use forecasting last to project one country-level arrivals series while interpreting it against hotel and stay indicators.

title: User Guide
text:
Data Scope
The current app uses two coordinated inputs:

Shared arrivals backbone: data/raw/visitor_arrivals_full_dataset.xlsx
Shared clustering artifacts: data/processed/clustering_country_wide.csv, data/processed/clustering_country_long.csv, data/processed/clustering_series_metadata.csv
Optional supporting tourism context: data/raw/Name your insight (4).xlsx

Country-level visitor arrivals remain the common analytical target across explorer, clustering, and forecasting. Hotel occupancy, average length of stay, number of hotels, and total room revenue are used only as optional supporting context for interpretation.

title: Shiny App Guide
text:
The Three Modules

1. Time Series Explorer
Purpose:

Select a monthly tourism series.
Inspect recent years of trend and volatility.
Review source, unit, and series identifier before analysis.



2. Cluster Prototype
Purpose:

Select several country-level arrival series.
Align them over a shared lookback window and cluster the trajectories.
Review silhouette quality, PCA separation, and cluster profiles.



3. Forecasting Prototype
Purpose:

Select a country-level monthly visitor-arrivals series.
Position that country series against hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and room revenue.
Set a train/test horizon.
Compare a seasonal-naive baseline with a forecasting model such as ARIMA or ETS.
Review both the forecast plot and the holdout accuracy table.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Forecasting Workflow
Following the sequence highlighted in Chapter 20, the prototype is organised into seven steps:

Import and inspect the selected monthly country-arrivals series.
Compare that country series with hotel and stay indicators.
Visualise the time path and seasonal structure.
Split the series into training and testing sets.
Fit a baseline and multiple forecasting models.
Compare testing-set accuracy.
Refit the best model and forecast forward.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 1: Visualise the Raw Time Series
text:
Step 1: Visualise the Raw Time Series
Chapter 19 emphasises that time-series work should begin with a direct inspection of trend, turning points, and volatility. The chart below shows the full monthly path of the selected tourism series.

ggplot(example_series, aes(x = date, y = value)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "#0f6b6f") +
  geom_point(size = 1.8, color = "#d86f45") +
  labs(
    title = example_label,
    subtitle = "Monthly tourism series used for forecasting",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Visitor arrivals (person)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Step 2: Check Seasonality and Decomposition
The next step is to confirm whether the series contains strong seasonal cycles and how the trend changed around the shock-and-recovery period. This mirrors the Chapter 19 emphasis on seasonal pattern analysis and decomposition.

Seasonal Pattern by Month

example_series |>
  mutate(
    month_lab = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE),
    year_num = year(date)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = month_lab, y = value, group = year_num, color = factor(year_num))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.65) +
  geom_point(size = 1.3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Seasonal Comparison by Month",
    subtitle = "Each coloured line represents one year",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Visitor arrivals (person)",
    color = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










STL-style Decomposition

example_tsibble <- example_series |>
  transmute(date = yearmonth(date), value = value) |>
  as_tsibble(index = date)

decomp_tbl <- example_tsibble |>
  model(STL(value)) |>
  components()

autoplot(decomp_tbl) +
  labs(
    title = "Trend / Seasonal / Remainder Decomposition",
    subtitle = "Used to explain the structural change before forecasting"
  )

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
section: Step 3: Create the Training and Testing Split
text:
Step 3: Create the Training and Testing Split
Following Chapter 20, the final 12 months are reserved as a holdout set. This keeps the evaluation time-aware and avoids random sampling.

split_summary <- tibble(
  segment = c("Training", "Testing"),
  start = c(min(forecast_results$training$date), min(forecast_results$testing$date)),
  end = c(max(forecast_results$training$date), max(forecast_results$testing$date)),
  n_obs = c(nrow(forecast_results$training), nrow(forecast_results$testing))
)

section: Step 4: Fit Baseline and Forecasting Models

Step 4: Fit Baseline and Forecasting Models
This prototype includes:

section: Step 6: Refit the Best Model and Forecast Forward
text:
Step 6: Refit the Best Model and Forecast Forward
After accuracy comparison, the best-performing model is refit on the full series and projected forward for the next 12 months. This is the final step highlighted in Chapter 20.

  plot_modeltime_forecast(
    .interactive = FALSE,
    .legend_max_width = 25
  ) +

    title = "Forward Forecast After Refit",
    subtitle = "Best model refit on the full monthly series"
  )

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Interpretation Notes
This forecasting prototype now aligns more closely with the course guidance:

It starts with time-series exploration rather than jumping straight to prediction.
It treats the data as an ordered monthly sequence.
It uses a proper holdout split based on time.
It compares a baseline with model-based approaches.
It uses model refitting to produce a future forecast path.

In presentation terms, the recommended narrative is:

Show the country-arrivals series together with hotel and stay indicators to position the source-market recovery in a broader tourism context.
Use the raw trend, seasonal plot, and decomposition to justify a forecasting approach.
Compare Seasonal Naive, ETS, and ARIMA on the same testing window.
Conclude with the best model and the forward projection.

title: Module Prototype: Forecasting
text:
Analytical Framing
The forecasting module uses a two-layer logic:

Core forecasting series: country-level monthly visitor arrivals.
Supporting performance indicators: hotel room occupancy rate, average length of stay, and total room revenue.

This means the forecasts answer the question “how may demand from each source market evolve?” while the supporting indicators help answer “does that demand recovery translate into broader tourism performance?”

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
7. Forecasting Module
The forecasting module will follow the Chapter 19 and 20 time-series workflow used in R for Visual Analytics. Users will first inspect the selected country-level monthly arrivals series through trend, seasonal, decomposition, and supporting-indicator views, then create a time-aware train/test split, and finally compare a baseline method with model-based forecasts on the same holdout window. A seasonal-naive forecast will act as the baseline, while ETS and ARIMA will provide the main model-based benchmarks through the modeltime workflow. Hotel occupancy, average length of stay, and room revenue will not be treated as separate forecasting targets in the final module; instead, they will help interpret whether forecast demand recovery aligns with broader tourism performance.
The expected outputs are:

a selectable target series
a raw time-series view with seasonal and decomposition diagnostics
a forecast plot showing training, test, and forecast paths
an accuracy table comparing RMSE, MAE, and MAPE
explanatory notes on the difference between the baseline and the forecasting model

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics EDA
text: Singapore’s tourism sector experienced a severe shock during the pandemic and entered an uneven recovery phase in the years that followed. Monthly patterns in visitor arrivals, hotel occupancy, length of stay, and the share of the Chinese source market all suggest distinct recovery stages. Static charts alone are insufficient for users who need to explore these structural shifts from multiple perspectives. This project therefore proposes an interactive visual analytics prototype to help users understand how Singapore’s tourism market moved from pandemic shock toward recovery and high-performance states.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics EDA
text:
Preparation

Package Insatllation & Loading

tidyverse- A family of modern R packages specially designed to support data science, analysis and communication task including creating static statistical graphs.
plotly - R library for plotting interactive statistical graphs.
patchwork - combining multiple ggplot2 graphs into one figure.
GGally - ‘GGally’ extends ‘ggplot2’ by adding several functions to reduce the complexity of combining geometric objects with transformed data
corrplot - a visual exploratory tool on correlation matrix that supports automatic variable reordering to help detect hidden patterns among variables.
readx1 - Graphical scales map data to aesthetics, and provide methods for automatically determining breaks and labels for axes and legends.
lubridate - provides intuitive functions for parsing, manipulating, and performing arithmetic on dates, times, and time zones


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, lubridate, plotly, patchwork, GGally, corrplot, readxl)

text:
EDA

EDA 1 - Tourism Trends in Singapore

data_clean$date <- as.Date(data_clean$date)

eda1 <- ggplot(
  data_clean,
  aes(
    x = date,
    y = visitor_arrivals / 1e6,
    color = period,
    group = period
  )
) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>Visitor Arrivals: ", round(visitor_arrivals / 1e6, 2), "M",
        "<br>Tourism Phase: ", period
      )
    ),
    linewidth = 1.1,
    alpha = 0.9
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "pre_covid" = "#2E8B57",
    "covid_shock" = "#E63946",
    "recovery" = "#457B9D"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Singapore Visitor Arrivals Trend",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
    color = "Tourism Phase"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(eda1, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Date",
      tickformat = "%Y-%m"
    )
  )





Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the average number of visitors arriving in Singapore was around 1.5 million per month. However, due to lockdowns and international travel restrictions during the pandemic period, tourism dropped sharply to nearly zero. Since 2022, the tourism sector has gradually recovered. Nevertheless, possibly due to broader economic conditions, the number of visitors arriving in Singapore remains slightly lower than the pre-pandemic level.


EDA 2 - China Market Dynamics in Singapore Tourism Recovery

data_clean$date <- as.Date(data_clean$date)

p1 <- ggplot(data_clean, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      y = visitor_arrivals / 1e6,
      color = "Total Visitors",
      group = "Total Visitors",
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>Total Visitors: ", round(visitor_arrivals / 1e6, 2), "M"
      )
    ),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      y = visitor_arrivals_china / 1e6,
      color = "China Visitors",
      group = "China Visitors",
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>China Visitors: ", round(visitor_arrivals_china / 1e6, 2), "M"
      )
    ),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Total Visitors" = "#457B9D",
    "China Visitors" = "#E63946"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "China Market Contribution to Singapore Tourism Recovery",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
    color = "Series"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p1, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Date",
      tickformat = "%Y-%m"
    )
  )




p2 <- ggplot(data_clean, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      y = china_share_pct,
      group = 1,
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>China Visitor Share: ", round(china_share_pct, 2), "%"
      )
    ),
    color = "#2E8B57",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "China Visitor Share Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "China Visitor Share (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


ggplotly(p2, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Date",
      tickformat = "%Y-%m"
    )
  )




eda2_combined <- p1 / p2

eda2_combined








This analysis compares total visitor arrivals with Chinese visitor arrivals and examines the share of Chinese visitors over time. Prior to the pandemic, Chinese visitors consistently accounted for a substantial portion of Singapore’s tourism demand and moved closely with overall visitor trends. During the COVID-19 shock in 2020–2021, both total and Chinese visitor arrivals declined sharply due to global travel restrictions. In the recovery period after 2022, total visitor arrivals rebounded more rapidly than Chinese visitor arrivals, indicating that Singapore’s tourism recovery was supported by multiple international markets rather than relying solely on the Chinese market.


EDA 3 - Tourism Demand and Hotel Utilisation

ggplot(
  data_clean,
  aes(x = visitor_arrivals/1e6, y = hotel_occ, color = period)
) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +

  geom_smooth(
    aes(group = 1),
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +

  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Tourism Demand and Hotel Utilisation",
    x = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
    y = "Hotel Occupancy (%)",
    color = "Tourism Phase"
  ) 








This analysis examines the relationship between visitor arrivals and hotel occupancy in Singapore. The scatter plot shows a clear positive relationship, where higher visitor arrivals are generally associated with higher hotel occupancy rates. During the COVID-19 shock period, visitor arrivals dropped sharply to near zero due to travel restrictions, while hotel occupancy declined but remained above 40–60%. This suggests that hotel demand was partially supported by domestic staycations or essential travel. As tourism gradually recovered after 2022, both visitor arrivals and hotel occupancy increased, moving closer to pre-pandemic levels. Overall, the results indicate that hotel utilisation closely follows tourism demand.


EDA 4 - Tourism Demand and Visitor Spending Behaviour

eda4 <- ggplot(
  data_clean,
  aes(
    x = visitor_arrivals/1e6,
    y = spend_per_capita,
    color = period,
    text = paste0(
      "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
      "<br>Visitor Arrivals: ", round(visitor_arrivals/1e6,2), "M",
      "<br>Spend Per Capita: $", round(spend_per_capita,0)
    )
  )
) +

geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +

geom_smooth(
  aes(group = 1),
  method = "lm",
  se = FALSE,
  color = "black",
  linewidth = 1.2
) +

scale_color_manual(values = c(
  "pre_covid" = "#2E8B57",
  "covid_shock" = "#E63946",
  "recovery" = "#457B9D"
)) +

labs(
  title = "Tourism Demand and Visitor Spending Behaviour",
  x = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
  y = "Spend Per Capita (SGD)",
  color = "Tourism Phase"
) +

theme_minimal()

ggplotly(eda4, tooltip = "text")





This analysis explores the relationship between visitor arrivals and tourism spending behaviour. The results indicate that higher visitor arrivals are generally associated with higher tourism spending levels. However, the recovery period shows variations in spending behaviour, suggesting that tourism recovery may not only depend on visitor volume but also on visitor spending patterns. Understanding these patterns is important for evaluating whether tourism recovery reflects a return to high-value tourism or simply an increase in visitor numbers.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics CDA
text: The exploratory data analysis (EDA) identified several key patterns in Singapore’s tourism data, including the sharp decline in visitor arrivals during the COVID-19 period and potential relationships between tourism indicators. Based on these observations, confirmatory data analysis (CDA) is conducted to statistically test these relationships.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics CDA
text:
Preperation
Within This analysis, a factor to be consider is periods. pre_covid represents the year 2017 to 2019 (End). Covid_shock Represents 2020-2021 (End), recovery represents Year 2022 - 2025(End)

Package Installation & Loading

tidyverse- A family of modern R packages specially designed to support data science, analysis and communication task including creating static statistical graphs.
plotly - R library for plotting interactive statistical graphs.
patchwork - combining multiple ggplot2 graphs into one figure.
GGally - ‘GGally’ extends ‘ggplot2’ by adding several functions to reduce the complexity of combining geometric objects with transformed data
corrplot - a visual exploratory tool on correlation matrix that supports automatic variable reordering to help detect hidden patterns among variables.
readx1 - Graphical scales map data to aesthetics, and provide methods for automatically determining breaks and labels for axes and legends.
lubridate - provides intuitive functions for parsing, manipulating, and performing arithmetic on dates, times, and time zones

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics CDA
text:
CDA (Confirmatory Data Analysis)

CDA 1 - Statistical Impact of COVID-19 on Visitor Arrivals
Did the COVID-19 pandemic significantly affect visitor arrivals to Singapore?
Hypothesis
H₀ (Null Hypothesis) There is no significant difference in visitor arrivals across the tourism periods.
H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis) Visitor arrivals differ significantly across the tourism periods.

plot_ly(
  data = data_clean,
  x = ~period,
  y = ~visitor_arrivals,
  color = ~period,
  type = "box",
  boxpoints = "outliers"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Visitor Arrivals Across Tourism Periods",
    xaxis = list(title = "Period"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )






anova_model <- aov(visitor_arrivals ~ period, data = data_clean)
summary(anova_model)

             Df    Sum Sq   Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)    
period        2 3.133e+13 1.566e+13   186.9 <2e-16 ***
Residuals   105 8.801e+12 8.382e+10                   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1








Note



The boxplot compares the distribution of visitor arrivals across different tourism periods. A substantial decline in the median visitor arrivals is observed during the COVID-19 period compared to the pre-COVID period, indicating a significant disruption to tourism demand. The interquartile range also shifts downward during the pandemic, suggesting that the overall level of visitor arrivals was consistently lower across most observations. In contrast, the recovery period shows an upward shift in the distribution, reflecting the gradual rebound of tourism activity after travel restrictions were lifted. Overall, the distributional differences across periods support the conclusion that the COVID-19 pandemic had a significant impact on Singapore’s tourism sector.




CDA 2 - Influence of Chinese Visitor Share on Total Visitor Arrivals
Does the share of Chinese visitors significantly influence total visitor arrivals to Singapore?
Hypothesis
H₀ (Null Hypothesis) China visitor share has no significant relationship with total visitor arrivals.
H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis) China visitor share significantly influences total visitor arrivals.

ggplot(data_clean, aes(x = china_share, y = visitor_arrivals)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between China Visitor Share and Total Visitor Arrivals",
    x = "China Visitor Share",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









model_cda2 <- lm(visitor_arrivals ~ china_share, data = data_clean)
summary(model_cda2)


Call:
lm(formula = visitor_arrivals ~ china_share, data = data_clean)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1133928  -612539   279205   479648   798720 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  1151022     103205   11.15   <2e-16 ***
china_share  -680992     486276   -1.40    0.164    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 609700 on 106 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.01817,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.008903 
F-statistic: 1.961 on 1 and 106 DF,  p-value: 0.1643








Tip



p-value > 0.05, H₀ (Null Hypothesis) Cannot be rejected


A linear regression analysis was conducted to examine the relationship between China’s visitor share and total visitor arrivals. The results indicate that the relationship is not statistically significant, as the coefficient for China’s visitor share is negative but insignificant (β = -680,992, p = 0.164). Since the p-value is greater than the 0.05 significance level, the null hypothesis cannot be rejected. This suggests that variations in China’s visitor share do not have a statistically significant impact on total visitor arrivals. Additionally, the low R-squared value ( \(R^2\) = 0.018) indicates that China’s visitor share explains only a small proportion of the variation in overall tourism demand.


CDA 3 - Seasonal Variation in Visitor Arrivals
Do visitor arrivals vary significantly across different months?
Hypothesis
H₀ (Null Hypothesis) Visitor arrivals do not differ significantly across months.
H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis) Visitor arrivals vary significantly across months, indicating seasonal tourism patterns.

data_clean <- data_clean %>%
  mutate(month = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE))
data_no_covid <- data_clean %>%
  filter(!(year %in% c(2020, 2021)))


p <- ggplot(
  data_no_covid,
  aes(
    x = month,
    y = visitor_arrivals,
    text = paste(
      "Month:", month,
      "<br>Visitor Arrivals:", format(visitor_arrivals, big.mark = ",")
    )
  )
) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "skyblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Seasonal Variation in Visitor Arrivals",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")






anova_month <- aov(visitor_arrivals ~ month, data = data_no_covid)

summary(anova_month)

            Df    Sum Sq   Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
month       11 8.784e+11 7.986e+10   0.525  0.881
Residuals   72 1.096e+13 1.522e+11               








Tip




The monthly distributions are based on observations from 2017–2025, with the COVID shock years (2020–2021) excluded to avoid distortion in seasonal patterns.
Pr(>F): Probability of observing an F value greater than the calculated F-statistic under the null hypothesis. (It is the p-value associated with the F-statistic.)



A one-way ANOVA test was conducted to examine whether visitor arrivals differ significantly across months. The analysis was performed using observations from 2017–2025, excluding the COVID shock years (2020–2021) to avoid distortion in seasonal patterns. The boxplot illustrates the distribution of visitor arrivals for each month, allowing potential seasonal variation to be visually assessed. However, the ANOVA results indicate that the effect of month on visitor arrivals is not statistically significant (F = 0.525, p = 0.881). Since the p-value is greater than the significance level of 0.05, the null hypothesis cannot be rejected. This suggests that visitor arrivals do not vary significantly across months in the dataset, indicating limited evidence of strong seasonal patterns in tourism demand during the analyzed period.

text: This review covers the clustering prototype draft in prototype/module-cluster.qmd, the supporting cleaned dataset, and the renderability of the updated Quarto page inside the full project.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Findings In The Original Draft

Finding 1: The page still grouped too much code into single blocks
The earlier revision improved reproducibility, but several sections still bundled too many steps together. That made the page harder to read in presentation mode and less faithful to the “one code block, one result, one explanation” rhythm requested for this prototype.


Finding 2: Package loading and data import were not shown explicitly in the first draft
The earlier draft loaded and transformed data in a hidden setup chunk. That made the page less aligned with the Habari Tanzania reference style, where the reader can see the workflow directly.


Finding 3: The stay-duration feature was underspecified
The draft proposed avg_stay_monthly directly for clustering, but the cleaned data preparation already created avg_stay_monthly_capped for modeling. Using the uncapped series would let a small number of pandemic months dominate Euclidean distances.


Finding 4: The choice of k = 3 needed evidence
The draft said “start with K = 3”, but it did not show the elbow pattern, silhouette diagnostics, or the interpretability tradeoff behind the final selection.


Finding 5: The page was not yet submission-ready
The original file was a short module note, not a full prototype report. It lacked a visible end-to-end analytical workflow, detailed results sections, and a stronger explanation layer after each output.


Finding 6: Some narrative statements were too static
After the restructuring, a few explanatory sentences still assumed fixed outcomes, such as which state was the largest. That could drift out of sync if the dataset changed later.


Finding 7: The centroid table labels could be misread
The cluster-center table displayed standardized values, but the column names did not make that explicit enough. A reader could incorrectly read them as raw monthly averages.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Fixes Implemented

Rewrote prototype/module-cluster.qmd again into smaller analytical blocks.
Changed the structure so one focused code chunk now produces one immediate figure or table.
Added a short explanation directly after each output instead of collecting explanations too late.
Kept package loading and data import visible.
Retained avg_stay_monthly_capped as the modeling stay variable.
Preserved the code-generated elbow, silhouette, cluster profile, and timeline outputs.
Replaced static statements with result-driven narrative where appropriate.
Renamed the centroid columns so they clearly indicate standardized values.
Kept the narrative aligned with the Habari Tanzania reference structure while using k-means appropriately for continuous monthly data.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Residual Risks

The selected k = 3 solution is substantively stronger than the k = 2 solution, but the silhouette score is slightly lower. This tradeoff should still be acknowledged in the final coursework report.
china_share can look high during collapse months because it is a relative measure. The final narrative should always pair it with absolute arrivals.
If additional monthly indicators are added later, the cluster labels may need to be recalibrated.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Review Outcome
No blocking issues remain in the updated clustering prototype. The page now matches the requested reproducible style much more closely: visible workflow, code-generated results, and explanation after each analytical step.

title: User Guide
text:
Step 2: Compare Country Recovery Patterns
Open the Cluster Prototype tab to:

Select a focused set of country-level visitor-arrivals series.
Set the year window and choose the normalization mode.
Pick a working k and run the clustering.
Read the module in order: Dashboard, Pattern Explorer, Focus Market in Context, Assignments.

The clustering unit is a country trajectory, not an individual month. The module is designed to answer which markets recovered in similar ways, not to assign each month into a market state.
